relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Elihu continued.
He asks for more patience because he still has words for God.
He claims to bring knowledge from afar and defend his Maker’s righteousness.
He presents himself as complete in knowledge.
The Divine is mighty and does not reject, Elihu says.
He links divine justice with the afflicted.
The righteous remain under divine sight and may be exalted.
Chains and affliction become the setting for instruction.
The hidden work and pride are disclosed.
The ear opens so a person may turn from harm.
Listening and service lead, in Elihu’s vision, to good days.
Refusal leads to ruin without knowledge.
A closed heart stores anger rather than cries for help.
Life can be wasted in youth.
Elihu sees affliction as a place where rescue and hearing can open.
He imagines Job drawn from distress into a broad, rich place.
But he warns that judgment still grips Job.
Wrath and ransom must not turn him aside.
Human strength cannot arrange escape from distress.
He warns against longing for the night that removes peoples.
He tells Job to guard himself from turning toward harm.
The Divine is exalted as unmatched teacher.
No one appoints God’s way or corrects him.
The work of the Divine is to be magnified in song.
Humanity sees it from afar.
God’s greatness exceeds knowing and counting.
Water is drawn up and becomes rain.
Clouds pour abundance on humanity.
Who can understand cloud-spreading and thunder?
Light spreads, and the sea’s roots are covered.
Through these, judgment and food both come.
Lightning fills the hands of the storm.
Thunder announces what is rising.
